w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Note for examiner: Word–based headings have been made so that navigation within the Word document is easy. To access the navigation panel, Control + F
Headings, is a good shortcut to use. Finally, zoom into more detail diagrams.
Metrics, Economic Buyer, Decision Criteria, Decision Process, Identify Pain, Champion
Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege

Sales training is effective but expensive and difficult to scale. Existing AI roleplay alternatives cannot reliably enforce structured sales methodologies. This project builds a chatbot that simulates a sales prospect, enabling salespeople to practise on–demand with no costs.

Three architectural approaches were prototyped and eliminated through empirical failure before settling on a hybrid architecture coupling a deterministic Finite State Machine for stage gating with a cloud LLM provider layer for natural language generation.

False stage advancement dropped from over 40% to under 8% following FSM implementation. UAT across 26 participants rated usability at 4.69/5. The system comprises approximately 6,600 lines of Python/HTML/JS and 1,050 lines of YAML, deployed at zero cost.

FSM stage gating combined with constrained LLM generation can enforce sales methodology adherence without fine–tuning or proprietary models, proving that separating control flow from content generation produces a more reliable training tool than probabilistic generation alone.

Without practice and instant feedback, sales representatives may develop unidentified bad habits that compound over time, negatively impacting a company's revenue. Human–led coaching is constrained by a 1:1 ratio: a 50–person team requires 50 concurrent sessions, making on–demand access logistically and financially prohibitive. This model is often unsustainable for SMEs, particularly given the well–documented limitations of passive, technology–delivered training as a standalone solution (Welsh et al., 2003).
The commercial scale of this gap is substantial:
Research on the science of training demonstrates that without reinforcement and active practice following initial

exposure, skills deteriorate rapidly. Training must be treated as an ongoing system rather than a discrete event, as what occurs after training is as critical as the training itself (Salas et al., 2012; Ebbinghaus, 1885; Murre and Dros, 2015).

Despite widespread recognition of the need for skill reinforcement, organisations consistently struggle to implement practice–based, application–focused training. Without structured needs assessment and evaluation at every stage, training failures and performance gaps go undetected (Attia et al., 2005).

Significant challenges remain in the content development, delivery and evaluation of sales training programmes. The intensified emphasis on accountability and rapidly changing sales roles makes it increasingly difficult for organisations to justify investment in training that lacks measurable, practice–based outcomes (Lassk et al., 2012).

While passive e–learning is cost–effective to deploy, it fundamentally compromises results with even completion rates below 15%. Crucially, passive formats severely degrade learning outcomes. A landmark meta–analysis of 225 studies found that learners in traditional, lecture–based environments were 55% more likely to fail than those engaged in active learning (Freeman et al., 2014, see figure below). Sales techniques must be honed under the cognitive load of a live objection. Simply observing a conversation is categorically different from actively participating in one.
The empirical case for active practice. Passive, observation–based learning (orange) consistently drives higher failure rates compared to active participation (blue). Source: Freeman et al. (2014).
This project presents a solution targeting three primary stakeholder cohorts:

require frequent application. Objection–handling degrades without low–stakes repetition against varied prospects, objections and conversational stages. An on–demand, non–judgemental roleplay system addresses this directly, building skill through volume independent of manager availability.
are capped by availability for practise and feedback frequency. During roleplays, they may not accurately portray the range of prospect personalities that the product or service demands.
require sales systems have appropriate adherence to brand and methodology consistency across various team–sizes to leverage large, reproducible performance data for informed decision–making.

The academic literature on conversational AI training is dominated by healthcare and customer service dialogue contexts, leaving sales methodology enforcement as a largely unexplored domain. Existing commercial solutions fall into three distinct categories: passive Learning Management Systems (e.g., Litmos, TalentLMS), retrospective call intelligence platforms (e.g., Gong, Chorus) and generative AI roleplay tools (e.g., Hyperbound, Second Nature).
Competitive analysis based on published product documentation

Table 1 reveals a consistent gap: no existing free or accessible solution combines active practice, live in–session feedback and deterministic methodology enforcement simultaneously. Generative competitors, such as Hyperbound, achieves some conversational authenticity through LLM generation, but this same mechanism prevents firm framework adherence. Manager–led roleplay achieves deterministic enforcement but is unscalable by definition.

The architectural design of this system must defend against root causes of “LLM drift” – LLMs’ diverging tendency to ignore custom instructions in favour of its original training over the course of a conversation.

First, models tuned via RLHF are trained to be highly agreeable and focused on overly user–pleasing behaviour (Bai et al., 2022). Although advantageous in some contexts, in a sales training context this is fatal; shown as premature collapse – is convinced too easily, instead of pushing back where needed. Hence, the model fails to sustain psychological resistance where necessary for realistic objection practice.

Secondly, even when reasoning steps are made explicit, like in agentic frameworks such as ReAct (Yao et al., 2023), the choice of which action to take next is still drawn from the model's prior training, not from the prompt – here termed as the “instruction–behaviour gap”. The prompt is simply just more tokens in the context window, competing for influence against the patterns reinforced during prior training. When the two disagree under semantic ambiguity, the prior usually wins: a methodology rule like
"do not pitch before establishing Cost of Inaction"
loses to the much stronger trained pattern of responding to a product question with a product answer. NEPQ (Acuff and Miner, 2023) requires a strict staged progressions:
Order is essential – if a salesperson tries to establish the “Pitch” before “COI”, the methodology fails. Generative models naturally collapse these phases due to their conversational priors.

Finally, real dialogue is disorderly: users are sarcastic, contradict themselves, soften disagreement with politeness and shift tone without warning. Schegloff (1992) characterises competent dialogue as one in which participants actively repair such breakdowns. LLMs without explicit repair scaffolding default to literal interpretation, miss the affective signal and let the conversation drift off–methodology with no mechanism to re–anchor it to a structured conversation.

The combined pressures of the above causes leads to the premature behavioural collapse. This design problem is therefore not just around simple dialogue generation, but regarding the effective enforcement of a non–negotiable, structured sequence against a model whose priors actively oppose it. Section 2.4 takes up this problem.

The system bifurcates sales interactions into Transactional or Consultative strategies based on product complexity and the degree to which buyer needs are already formed. The Transactional model suits straightforward purchases where requirements are explicit, prioritising efficiency and specification over discovery. The Consultative model applies where challenges are not yet fully articulated and the value of a solution carries inherent uncertainty, requiring deeper exploration before any offering is made.

For consultative selling, the NEPQ framework (Acuff & Miner, 2023) was selected because its staged questioning sequence provides a transferable structure for sales training. Additionally, NEPQ operates consistently with established behavioural principles: its emphasis on self–generated reasoning aligns with motivational interviewing, where eliciting self–discovered insight is prioritised over directive instruction (Miller and Rollnick, 2012) and its focus on consequence–framing reflects loss aversion, whereby potential losses carry greater psychological weight than equivalent gains (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979). BANT and MEDDIC were also considered but rejected as neither prescribes Socratic questioning nor psychological stage sequencing, making deterministic enforcement challenging.
Full framework details are provided in the NEPQ Appendix.
The solution had to be entirely cost–free. Given the scale and budget constraints of the final project, this was non–negotiable from the outset.

A core functionality to include was a role–play system that works both ways. Users can play the prospect to understand the buyer's perspective, or step into the salesperson's shoes for live practise. Here, the AI should be programmed with different personality types to simulate the varying scenarios salespeople face in the real world.
These interactions had to occur in real time. Any latency or delay would break the natural conversational flow, undermining the practise value entirely.

An analysis component for these roleplays with beyond surface–level feedback actually allows salespeople to identify non–conducive habits they may be oblivious. Building on the existing model, a performance tracking component done continuously across individual profiles captures metrics at different stages of the conversation to deliver targeted feedback rather than generic observations so salespeople can work on highest leverage actions.
Finally, a personal analytics layer is beneficial: a way to track individual progress over time, giving each user a clear picture of where they stand and how they are developing across sessions.
The full requirements table is provided in Appendix A.
Figure 2: Use Case Diagram – scope boundary and actor interactions for the Sales Roleplay Chatbot system

After understanding the market positioning, an Extreme Programming (XP) approach was adopted to mitigate initial architectural uncertainty. Generative AI was utilised to assemble initial skeletons, allowing for refactoring and immediate empirical testing of the NEPQ framework. While this enabled rapid conceptual validation, early iterations produced a high degree of architectural coupling, serving merely as inspiration.

The initial strategy of a local Qwen 2.5–1.5B model had two fatal categories of failure. First, hardware constraints below a 6GB VRAM threshold imposed an unfeasible 300–second latency. Second, the model demonstrated insufficient neural capacity to resolve multi–constraint instructions. This manifested as “instruction bleed” (defined by ElevenLabs, 2024), where the model failed to distinguish between system–level methodology and conversational inputs. The ability to manage these competing priorities is strictly limited by parameter count (Kaplan et al., 2020). Consequently, the system consistently abandoned its persona or ignored the sales framework. Due to these compounding limitations in hardware and cognitive stability, the localized model was discarded for a deterministic cloud architecture.
To eliminate Phase 1's per–turn LLM latency, the system switched to keyword matching for stage advancement. Responses became instant, but the logic broke.

was treated as confirmed intent. A single word like "want" anywhere in a message immediately set desired_outcome. One bot acknowledgment ("Got it") then satisfied both conditions in the advancement check, pushing INTENT → LOGICAL in just two turns (before budget, urgency, or decision authority had been established).
# chatbot.py (initial iteration) – extraction and advancement crammed in one class
if any(word in user_lower for word in ["need", "want", "looking for", "help with"]):
has_signal = any(sig in bot_response.lower() for sig in ["got it", "makes sense"])
return has_signal and has_data # Two turns → advance

This violated NEPQ's core requirement: each stage must probe and confirm understanding across multiple turns before progressing. Instead, every subsequent stage was built on unverified assumptions. A casual "I'd want something affordable" became the system's authoritative signal that intent was established. The failure cascaded: the LLM was prompted as though a genuine need had been surfaced, when in reality none had been explored. February’s implementation of a declarative FSM,
config extraction and higher cohesion functions was the step in the right direction for an overall structural fix.
Having eliminated approaches from Section 2.1, four further architectural options were evaluated and discarded before committing to the final design:
Option A – Pure LLM (prompt–only, no control structure)

The simplest approach: instruct the LLM via a long system prompt to follow the NEPQ framework. This was prototyped in the first week and discarded immediately because without hard guards, it experiences significant drift.

Its Reasoning and Acting (ReAct) loop uses probabilistic routing, prioritising semantic relevance over structural constraints, so any mention of pricing or features is likely to trigger the "Pitch" tool and bypass the mandatory Logical and Emotional stages. Because stage transitions happen inside the model's inference rather than in explicit code, the execution path cannot be enforced or verified, making defects difficult to isolate.

Training a custom model on labelled sales conversations was considered but discarded due to high computational resource requirements and time constraints. Furthermore, there was the necessity of trial–and–error retraining for optimisations in which the scope of this project did not prove as the best use of resources as there are alternatives of existing models. Also, there was no sufficiently labelled dataset found during extensive research for NEPQ–specific conversations and creating such would not be the appropriate usage of time too.
Option D: Keep external LLM provider but use strategy pattern

The Strategy pattern failure is the most intellectually interesting rejection because it initially looks correct. The explanation makes two precise arguments: (1) strategies cannot be stateless across NEPQ phases without reintroducing the coupling they were meant to eliminate; and (2) the pattern controls
transitions happen, that is precisely a state machine's job. This is the kind of design reasoning the mark scheme's 70–79% band rewards.
The system context diagram shows a clear separation of concerns

The app communicates via REST JSON over HTTPS, so it can be tested with any browser or tool. Flask was chosen for its simplicity as the project only needs basic routing and heavier frameworks like Django would introduce unnecessary overhead.
Sessions are kept in memory for fast access and saved to disk after each message. If the server restarts, sessions are restored from disk on the next request so that no data is lost.

The diagram below traces a single user message end–to–end: the client sends a request, the system builds a prompt, the LLM generates a response and FSM logic validates it before returning it to the client.
UML Sequence Diagram – Lifecycle of a single chat turn across Prompt Constraints (Layer 1), FSM Guards (Layer 2) and Output Contracts (Layer 3).
This is like a blueprint of all the building blocks in the code – what they are called, what data they hold and how they connect to each other.
Figure 4: UML Class Diagram – Core module hierarchy showing SalesChatbot (orchestrator), SalesFlowEngine (FSM), BaseLLMProvider hierarchy (Strategy pattern) and supporting DTOs.

TO note, initially, this started as a strategy pattern, thinking it was the ideal way for switching between the different stages of selling, but it wouldn’t take context ideally from previous strategies as they were created for independence. When they would, then coupling and repetition was introduced, violating DRY principles.
The FSM enforces progression on discrete, predefined signals but is semantically blind: it cannot read emotional shifts, sarcasm, or contextual contradiction. On its own, it can hold the
of what is generated within each stage. No single mechanism closes every gap, so the architecture deploys three independent layers to catch what each previous one misses.
Shape model behaviour by binding it to a defined role and stage before generation.
Probabilistic, not enforced; instructions weaken under ambiguity or long context.
Enforce stage order; block transitions until a valid signal is observed.
Operates on predefined signals only; cannot judge semantic intent.
Validate response structure after generation; reject distorted output before it reaches the user.
Acts post–hoc; cannot prevent flawed internal reasoning, only its surfacing.

The layers are deliberately independent, so failure in one does not cascade into the next. A prompt that drifts mid–generation cannot disrupt stage progression, because the FSM holds the current stage until a valid signal is confirmed; and a structurally invalid response is intercepted by the output contract before the user sees it. Even before drift can happen, the prompt rules are designed to prevent it anyway.
For the Voice Mode features, the core design principle is
: voice and typed input converge into the same text–based chatbot pipeline, ensuring consistent follow–on actions (state tracking, response generation) regardless of input method.

Most important design choice was creating the clear separation of concerns. This modular separation allows voice processing to fail or evolve independently, without affecting core conversation logic. As a result, every voice command takes a five–stage sequential pipeline built on a layered architecture:

Deepgram was selected for streaming transcription with low–latency speech–to–text performance, enabling real–time voice interaction without perceptible delays, but with straightforward API integration, while maintaining high transcription accuracy across diverse accents and speech patterns.
Deepgram also has useful built–in capabilities like enabling “smart_format” to provide formatting and normalisation (e.g., punctuation, phone numbers, currency).
STT and TTS fallback systems mitigate risk of reliance on sole providers

The system's security posture is governed by a single principle: defence–in–depth proportionate to the deployment context. This is a single–instance academic tool with no persistent user store and no financial data. Full enterprise hardening would be both architecturally disproportionate and incompatible with the zero–cost, low–latency requirements. Controls were therefore selected to eliminate the most probable attack vectors, with residual risks explicitly accepted rather than overlooked.

All security primitives are centralised in backend/security.py. Route handlers consume them through decorators and middleware rather than inline logic – one file to audit is simpler to verify than checks scattered across multiple route files.

was conducted using STRIDE; “the most mature threat modeling method” (Shevchenko et al., 2018), mapping each threat category to a specific attack vector before selecting a mitigation. The full threat matrix is documented in the Security Appendix.
PromptInjectionValidator – regex strips known adversarial phrases silently
InputValidator – messages over 1,000 characters rejected at ingress
SecurityHeadersMiddleware – X–Frame–Options, X–Content–Type–Options injected on every response

Injection phrases are replaced with the literal string [removed] rather than rejected outright. Outright rejection signals to an attacker that a filter triggered; silent replacement removes the attack without providing that feedback.

Two residual risks are consciously accepted: the absence of a structured audit trail (appropriate for an academic tool, not a production one) and the lack of full RBAC (the system operates in a single–user–role context). Both are documented in the Security Appendix alongside the complete STRIDE matrix and verification procedures.

The LLM Provider module manages the connection between the application and third–party language model APIs (e.g., Groq, SambaNova). This Section explains how the system uses abstraction to prevent dependency on a single provider and to guarantee continuous operation during API outages.

Initially, the chatbot was connected to just one API provider (Groq) and experienced service interruptions. Groq's free–tier rate–limits were hit over 3 conversations in manual testing to explicitly flag the issue. This is a high–impact and high–probability risk as it completely stops response generation mid–conversation until API–limit reset and Groq’s rate limits may change anytime.
The Solution: Abstract Interface with a Factory

To resolve this, the system was restructured to connect to a shared interface called BaseLLMProvider. Any provider that implements this interface can be used interchangeably, without the rest of the application being aware of which one is active.
Figure 5: The chatbot orchestrator depends only on the shared interface, remaining unaware of which specific API resolves its request at runtime.

This structure follows the Strategy design pattern (Gamma et al., 1994): A dedicated Factory pattern handles provider selection at instantiation. The SalesChatbot remains provider–indifferent, as it receives its configuration passed via the constructor rather than declaring a static, hardcoded reference to a specific LLM provider.

The chatbot defines an automatic fallback order upon failure in chatbot.py (On each user message, if the active provider returns an error (e.g., a rate limit or timeout), the system automatically attempts the next provider in the list before returning an error to the user. Both times Groq's rate limits were hit during the project, SambaNova handled the request transparently – no manual intervention was needed and the user saw no interruption. Additionally, the Groq provider itself rotates between two API keys (SAFE_GROQ_API_KEY and ALTERNATIVE_GROQ_API_KEY) on rate–limit errors before escalating to the cross–provider fallback.

Moreover, this accommodates future extensibility: additional providers or LLM modules can be integrated by implementing the existing interface, requiring no extensive refactoring of the core chatbot logic. This design mitigates risks associated of:
Tight coupling to one provider and model
External API dependency; strict rate limits
Alternative free–tier cloud; no rate issues observed in testing
Zero latency; no network or API key requirement
Returns a fixed string (no actual LLM reasoning)
Zero latency; Validates prompt variants are different
No LLM reasoning; output is raw prompt data
All source code was managed through a Git repository on GitHub across two branches. master was the stable, deployment–ready version connected to Render for automatic redeployment on merge

. A separate working branch isolated all active development, meaning experimental changes such as the Prospect Mode and evaluation modules could be built and verified without risking the live application.
During the final refinement phase, structured commit messages were adopted (e.g., fix(fsm):, refactor(diagrams):) to document the purpose of each change against a specific system component.

All development proceeded through an isolated working branch, with systematic code quality improvements tracked and verified. For example, a single audit identified nine instances of dead code–an entire duplicate training/quiz.py module plus seven orphaned methods across SalesChatbot, SessionPersistence, ProspectSession and PerformanceTracker–systematized into one commit with explicit test verification: "all 336 tests pass." This removed 1,148 lines of net code while maintaining zero regression risk, demonstrating rigorous engineering discipline over feature volume.
A Trello Kanban board managed technical direction across six active columns, providing backlog visualisation and enabling rapid reprioritisation when needed.
Figure 6: Trello board nearing project close

Task generation was driven by retrospective analysis rather than upfront assumption. At each sprint boundary, emergent technical risks identified through developer testing were converted directly into explicit deficiency cards (e.g., "Documentation for previous methods" and "Eval & reflection = Missing"). This ensured the backlog reflected the true,

This agility was critical during the core architectural pivot. When the Strategy Pattern proved unable to enforce stage–by–stage rules, the backlog was aggressively restructured. The blocking ticket "Strategy switch entanglement" was moved to DISCARDED and logged as "SWITCHED TO FSM". Backed by project diary metrics–which recorded a massive reduction in execution logic from 855 to 319 LOC–this clean–up ensured the board remained strictly aligned with the new FSM architecture, preventing the accumulation of dead legacy tasks.

Supervisor meetings served as structured review points at key development stages – full notes are in Appendix A. Rather than following a fixed schedule, sessions were called when a decision needed validation before work could continue. Key outcomes included specifying the system architecture in October and formally adopting the FSM approach in February after a live demo revealed a fundamental limitation in the Strategy Pattern.
Three static analysis tools were applied consistently to maintain code integrity across modules
Type mismatches and unsafe module interfaces
Unused imports, whitespace violations, style inconsistencies
Deviations from Python's official style specification

Sensitive credentials were stored exclusively in local .env files, excluded from version control via .gitignore. Periodic full–project archives were saved to a 2FA–protected Google Drive. Three independent backup layers were maintained throughout development:

A modular design was achieved after first categorising segments of code by SLOC, functional relationships and shared variables. Strategic brainstorming against common design patterns and refactoring techniques assisted with design–decision making. For modularity retention, additions were thought of – “if a feature could not be lifted out cleanly, that was evidence of hidden coupling”, so the boundary was redrawn before the code was written. This enforced the SRP concretely.
turn dispatch, provider calls, session persistence
Depends on all core modules; must
contain prompt logic or FSM rules directly
stage definitions, guard functions, advancement logic
Independent control layer; must not depend on LLM providers or prompt assembly
Pure configuration layer; no runtime state or decision logic
intent classification, guardedness detection, keyword extraction
Stateless analysis layer; cannot mutate FSM or session state
Depends on prompts.py + analysis.py only; no FSM transitions allowed
Sandbox module; must not affect production FSM state
Configuration–only layer; no runtime decision–making allowed
Read–only access to FSM state; cannot trigger transitions
Top–level entry point; only module allowed to manage request lifecycle
Isolated pre–processing layer; cannot access chatbot state or LLM providers
Consistent with PEP 257 conventions, each module contains a high–level docstring summarising its exported objects and purpose.
The consultative flow maps directly to the NEPQ methodology as follows:
Intent detection, elicitation statements, 6–turn safety valve
Stage–specific pitch template, anti–permission rules, assumptive close
The SalesChatbot orchestrator (chatbot.py) is a high–level controller facilitating the below flow:
This orchestrator focuses on control flow and delegates lower–level logic (e.g., prompt construction and transition rules) to their respective modules:

The provider call and FSM advancement are synchronous – the ChatResponse cannot be returned until _apply_advancement completes. Session persistence and analytics are dispatched on detached daemon threads so I/O latency cannot block the user response. The in–memory history is authoritative for the live session; the on–disk JSON is a replay artefact, not a recovery log. Session identity is carried by a UUID assigned at Flask–layer init and stored on the SalesChatbot instance – one instance per active session, held in the Flask session registry.

turn–pairs from the FSM's conversation_history and rolls current_stage back using a snapshot stack maintained per advancement. It does not re–invoke the provider, so the operation is O(1) with no LLM cost.

generate_training() bypasses the chat pipeline entirely: it reads the completed conversation_history post–hoc and dispatches it to training_coach.score_session(), reusing only the analysis layer – reaching neither the FSM nor the provider.
Three important methods are used for state transitions in the FSM:

acts as the transition gateway. It reads the current stage's advance_on parameter from the config, runs the corresponding guard function and returns the next stage name if the guard passes or None to hold the current stage
performs the state update: it writes the new stage to self.current_stage and resets self.stage_turn_count to zero, locking the next LLM call into the new prompt context.

adapts the flow mid–conversation. If the user signals a specific budget or product early, the engine shifts to a collapsed 3–stage Transactional flow, bypassing the full Logical and Emotional discovery sequence.
Complete end–to–end flow of FSM’s different states and transitionary Guard checks

Guard functions control when transitions are permitted. user_shows_doubt() governs Logical → Emotional, passing only when the user explicitly acknowledges their current approach is failing. user_expressed_stakes() governs Emotional → Pitch, passing only when the user articulates personal or financial consequences of inaction. Each stage also carries a turn cap – if a user fails to organically trigger a guard, the cap forces progression to prevent the conversation stalling.
Guards are bound to config strings via a static ADVANCEMENT_RULES dictionary, avoiding complex nested conditionals:
if rule_name and rule_name in ADVANCEMENT_RULES:

A key problem this solves: naive keyword matching would cause false–positive transitions – for example, advancing stage because a user said "I am not struggling." To prevent this, guard evaluation uses word–boundary regex with a retrospective negation window. For each keyword match, the three preceding words are checked against a negation list. If any are negating terms, the match is discarded and the stage is held:
if any(w in DEFAULT_NEGATIONS for w in window):
This ensures a stage only advances on a semantically affirmative signal from the user.
Specifically, here is how four exemplary drift categories have been attempted to handle:
– premature advancement along the NEPQ sequence – is held by the guard logic in Section 3.1.3; the prompt layer reinforces it by assembling pitch context only when current_stage == Stage.PITCH.

– user–introduced tangents – is caught pre–generation by analysis.py:extract_user_keywords(), which injects a hidden course–correction directive when deviation from stage goals exceeds threshold. This implements the Schegloff repair mechanism established in Section 1.4.

– casual inquiry misclassified as buying signal – is gated by an explicit Intent classifier in flow.py that pre–labels each turn HIGH/MED/LOW before prompt assembly, closing the “instruction–behaviour gap” introduced in Section 1.4.
– persona decay over long sessions – is countered in content.py by lexical entrainment and a persona checkpoint every PERSONA_CHECKPOINT_TURNS = 6.

The prompt assembly engine constructs a deterministic, stage–aware system prompt on every conversational turn, ensuring consistent LLM behaviour without reliance on static prompts or long–range context accumulation. This design directly addresses variability and drift by enforcing a structured input pipeline.

Prompt construction is performed in content.py via generate_stage_prompt(), which composes a complete system prompt before each dispatch to the model. Construction follows a deterministic 11–component pipeline ordered deliberately for positional bias, for example primacy bias
A simplified view of the assembly:
state = pre_state if pre_state is not None else analyse_state(history, user_message, signal_keywords=SIGNALS)
override = check_override_condition(base, user_message, stage, history, preferences)
stage_prompt, stage_context = _get_stage_specific_prompt(strategy, stage, state, user_message, history, objection_data)
# Tier 6: Turn metadata calculation (needed for terse/checkpoint logic)
# Tier 7: Terse guidance (CONDITIONAL, not just present)
terse_guidance = "\nTERSE INPUT: Very short answer. Make ONE observation, then ONE question. Do not over–probe.\n"
# Tier 8: Persona checkpoint (CONDITIONAL, not just present)
persona_checkpoint = f"\n[CHECKPOINT – Turn {turn_count}]: Stay in {strategy} mode. One question per turn. Current stage: {stage}.\n"
# Tier 9: History block (CONDITIONAL on include_history flag)
# Tier 10: State block with turn metadata
# Final assembly: Deliberate Order (ordering creates positional bias effects)
The full 11–component pipeline and the conditions governing each component's inclusion are summarised below:
Factual product/strategy context; establishes hard constraints
Short–circuits on direct info request, soft positive at pitch, or excessive validation
Early exit if triggered; nothing else runs
Tone alignment – verbose, concise, or neutral register
Always; precedes stage logic so adaptation informs the FSM instruction
Interaction–style adaptation: decisive user, low intent, guarded, question fatigue
Always; positioned after tactics so adaptation already frames it
Objection SOP block (type, reframe strategy, step–by–step flow)
Objection stage only; empty string otherwise
"Don't over–probe" constraint for very short user replies
If input word count < TERSE_INPUT_THRESHOLD and stage != intent
Turn metadata: stage, strategy, turn count, intent level, guarded flag
Always – appended last for maximum recency bias
Stage–specific instructions are stored in a two–dimensional nested dictionary keyed first by strategy, then by stage:
The get_prompt(strategy, stage) accessor performs two chained dictionary lookups – O(1) – and logs a warning on a cache miss, degrading gracefully to an empty string rather than raising.
A known failure mode of instruction–tuned LLMs is verbatim echo: repeating the user's input as a preamble before adding content. SHARED_RULES addresses this with a dedicated ANTI–PARROTING block:
embed 1–2 keywords (nouns, adjectives) from the user's message, not the full phrase.
never replay more than 3 consecutive words from the user's prior message.

Manual testing showed rule statements alone were insufficient – the model requires concrete examples to distinguish compliant from non–compliant output. Few–Shot Contrastive Examples were therefore adopted, pairing each rule with inline GOOD/BAD responses:
Core constraint strings decoupled from application logic
, enforcing the P1/P2/P3 execution priority order.
Maps the active FSM state to its instruction set, decoupled from runtime execution logic
Global constraint injection across all prompts
Enforces universal behavioural boundaries (e.g., anti–parroting, tone constraints) regardless of stage
Controls response verbosity via discrete policy outputs based on pre–classified intent

Decoupling prompt material from the execution engine (chatbot.py, content.py) enforces modularity means modifying a stage's linguistic constraints cannot introduce regressions in the application state manager.
BEFORE RESPONDING internal reasoning checklists (shared rules + per–phase block).
Brown et al. (2020); Liu et al. (2023)
Grounded GOOD/BAD paired examples preventing state hallucinations in each stage payload.
Initial persona assignment and the periodic [CHECKPOINT – Turn N] checkpoint anchor.
Explicit structural limits and anti–formatting rules (e.g., forbidding Markdown fences).
The immutable P1/P2/P3 priority tiers establishing deterministic rule resolution bounds.
The GROUNDING RULES block enforcing exact–match price quoting and banning estimation.
Weng et al. (2022); Wang et al. (2022)
The CHECK criteria anchored at the termination of each phase prompt.

A key architectural decision is the separation of configuration from execution logic. YAML externalises behaviour–defining content including keyword lists, thresholds, objection flows, personas, product data and prompt fragments, while Python handles deterministic control flow, validation and prompt assembly. This means most behavioural changes, such as refining signal vocabularies, objection patterns, or rubric content, can be made through YAML edits alone without touching core business logic.

YAML files are mainly either support deterministic runtime logic such as signal detection and thresholds, or provide prompt fragments and instruction blocks used during prompt construction. The table below maps each subsystem to its YAML source and primary handler modules:
Prompt blocks are assembled in a deterministic sequence and high–priority overrides can short–circuit normal assembly.
Reduces ambiguity and makes prompt behaviour easier to reason about and test.
Different YAML files serve different modules and responsibilities, so
Similarly named concepts are interpreted within their own module context.
Seller and prospect modes use shared product data differently: the seller receives direct product context, while the prospect receives buyer–framed context.
Preserves simulation realism and avoids leaking authoritative sales information into the buyer role.
YAML files are loaded through cached loader functions and returned as defensive copies.
Improves efficiency while reducing accidental shared–state mutation.

Prospect mode inverts the standard conversational roles: the user practises as the salesperson and the LLM is constrained to behave as a realistic buyer. The core design challenge this creates is consistency as an unconstrained LLM will improvise a buyer persona differently each turn, breaking the simulation's internal logic. The solution is to bind all mutable buyer state to a Python dataclass and rebuild the LLM's system prompt from that state on every turn, keeping the LLM's behaviour anchored to the same persona throughout the session.

On initialisation, ProspectSession selects a persona using select_persona(), which draws randomly from a product–specific pool defined in prospect_config.yaml (covering domains such as luxury cars, b2b_saas, fitness, insurance and others), falling back to general–purpose personas when the product type is unrecognised. Each persona is a structured dictionary specifying the buyer's name, professional background, known needs, pain points, budget ceiling and personality style:
background: "Operations lead scaling a 60–person services company"
personality: "Pragmatic evaluator, asks direct ROI and implementation questions"

These attributes are injected into the LLM's system prompt by _build_system_prompt(), which populates a template with persona details. The persona details a discrete label chosen by mapping the current readiness score to one of five phrases and difficulty–level behaviour rules, for example, a score around 0.5 maps to "On the fence – some interest but not sold" . Because the system prompt is rebuilt each turn from the current ProspectState, the LLM's contextual awareness stays synchronised with the simulation's internal state without requiring the LLM to track any of it independently.

Readiness scale is from 0.0 to 1.0. The terminal flags of has_committed and has_walked are set at most once, making session termination an irreversible state transition rather than a condition re–evaluated each turn. This prevents readiness from dropping back below the threshold after a weak closing attempt and re–opening a session that had already resolved.

The three difficulty levels encode distinct buyer archetypes. Buyer typology research (Merrill & Reid, 1981) (Dixon & Adamson, 2011) establishes that buyers differ systematically in resistance patterns and decision styles, justifying the use of distinct
profiles. The specific parameter values below were calibrated empirically through testing

Difficulty modulates these parameters deterministically without LLM involvement; the LLM's injected behaviour rules vary in parallel (e.g., a Hard prospect is instructed to "challenge claims, use comparison leverage and give short non–committal answers initially").

At each turn, after the LLM has generated the prospect's response, _score_sales_message() combines rule–based keyword matching via contains_nonnegated_keyword() (described in Section 3.1.3) with intent classification via classify_intent_level(), which is the same deterministic message rater used in the seller–mode FSM (Section 3.1.3). This avoids a second LLM call per turn and the inconsistent judgements that it would produce.
return 1 # Dominant negative – short–circuits all other rules
score += 0.3 # Simple heuristic rewarding engagement; false–positive rate
# (e.g. objection–framed questions) is acceptable at academic scope
if any(w in msg_lower for w in ["price", "cost", "payment", "buy", "purchase"]):

Signal lists are loaded from signals.yaml – the same vocabulary source that governs FSM guard functions throughout the main chatbot (Section 3.1.6), keeping keyword definitions consistent across both modes. The 1–5 rating is then converted to a readiness delta by _update_readiness():

A neutral message (rating = 3) produces a small positive delta of 0.01 rather than zero; this prevents sessions from stalling when neither side advances or retreats, ensuring readiness always trends toward a resolution. Since gain and loss vary by difficulty (Easy:

0.12 / 0.05; Hard: 0.06 / 0.10), the same message quality produces asymmetric readiness shifts: a rating of 5 yields +0.24 on Easy but only +0.12 on Hard; a rating of 1 costs −0.10 on Easy but −0.20 on Hard. This asymmetry is hardcoded via the gain and loss constants, making difficulty separation precise and fully deterministic.
After every turn, _check_end_conditions() evaluates two terminal outcomes:

The sold path requires both a readiness threshold (≥ 0.85) and a minimum turn count of 3. These values were set empirically: 0.85 requires sustained positive scoring rather than a single spike and the three–turn floor prevents a single strong commitment keyword in the opening message from ending the session before any real exchange has occurred. The walked path fires on either patience expiry or complete disengagement (readiness ≤ 0.0). Both branches set the corresponding terminal flag on ProspectState, after which further processing in process_turn() is suppressed.

Once a session terminates, get_evaluation() delegates to evaluate_prospect_session() in prospect_evaluator.py, submitting the full transcript and outcome to the LLM for retrospective assessment across five weighted criteria:

Needs discovery is weighted highest because NEPQ methodology treats uncovering a buyer's problems as the prerequisite for every subsequent stage. A salesperson who pitches without discovering needs cannot be said to have sold well regardless of the outcome. The weighted total produces an overall score (0–100) and letter grade (F–A), with per–criterion written feedback. Note the deliberate contrast with seller mode (Section 3.3.2), where post–session scoring is entirely deterministic: seller mode has discrete FSM events to count, while prospect mode requires qualitative judgement of a criteria.
An optional per–turn coaching hint can be generated by a single LLM call if show_hints is enabled to provide the user with a suggested next sales move without affecting the readiness score.
As shown in the diagram, user–submitted product data is cleaned and injected into the model's context.
Diagram illustrates the one–way journey of custom product data. Both modes are slightly different
The system is designed not to trust the user’s input format. Before saving the text to a file, clean_value() forces the text through strict rules:
Replaces multiple spaces or tabs with a single space.
Replaces large gaps (3 or more blank lines) with just a normal paragraph break.

It forcefully cuts the text off at a hard character limit (MAX_FIELD_LENGTH). Additionally, if the user submits a data field that is not in the approved list (ALLOWED_FIELDS), the system deletes it. This prevents pasting of a massive document that crashes the AI's memory limits.

AI models read plain text much faster than they read nested code structures (like JSON or dictionaries). Instead of handing the AI a dictionary, get_custom_knowledge_text() reads the saved data and formats it into a normal text string by combining the field name (key) and its content into single lines. Lists are automatically formatted with bullet points.
The sequence diagram illustrates the flow; the implementation uses two isolation strategies. In
, custom product data is wrapped and passed directly:
In prospect.py, the same pattern applies but with a framing instruction to prevent the model from assuming prior technical knowledge:

f"––– BEGIN CUSTOM PROSPECT DATA –––
{prospect_knowledge}
––– END CUSTOM PROSPECT DATA –––
"
"(You may know some of this as buyer research–do not assume full technical knowledge.)"
knowledge = f"{knowledge}\n\n{custom_block}" if knowledge else custom_block

The delimiters (––– BEGIN/END –––) prevent instruction bleed by creating a hard semantic boundary that signals to the model: "treat this as factual context, not commands." Token safety is enforced upstream in clean_value() (knowledge.py), which strips injection patterns and truncates at MAX_FIELD_LENGTH before the data ever reaches the prompt assembly pipeline. This is "defense in depth"–filtering injection attempts at ingestion prevents them from ever reaching the LLM.
The system treats factual–identification questions differently than more subjective–based ones. Three functions handle this accordingly.
evaluate_stage_quiz is entirely local. It strips whitespace, lowercases the input and tests it against the expected keyword. Both stage and strategy identification must pass:

evaluate_next_move_quiz handles live sales lines – responses code cannot reliably score. The input is sent to the LLM alongside the stage's YAML rubric. The returned score is clamped to 0–100; the alignment field is forced to one of three values only: strong, partial, or weak.

evaluate_direction_quiz tests strategic foresight. Rather than a pass/fail, the LLM returns two arrays – key_concepts_got and key_concepts_missed – exposing exactly which psychological triggers were or were not deployed.
The Training Coach runs three sub–systems with distinct responsibilities:

generate_training() injects the current stage's YAML rubric (goal, advance_when) into a system prompt alongside the latest exchange. The LLM returns a three–field JSON object: what_happened (≤15 words), next_move (≤15 words) and watch_for (list, ≤8 words each). Word limits are enforced post–parse by _truncate_words(), so the constraint holds regardless of model output. On LLM failure the fields are populated directly from the rubric – no second API call.

answer_training_question() builds a richer prompt: the last eight turns, current stage rubric including key_concepts and the active methodology (NEPQ or NEEDS→MATCH→CLOSE). Without altering the underlying context, the caller selects from three coaching styles:
Socratic (one Socratic question back to the trainee, helping them come up with the answer)
score_session() avoids the LLM entirely; all five dimensions are computed deterministically from the analytics event log:
Sessions that exceed the ideal length by 1–3 turns incur a 20% penalty instead of plummeting. This is a gradual penalty rather than a sharp drop to 50%.

All constants are externalised to SCORING_RUBRIC in constants.py. The governing principle is consistent: any evaluation resolvable with deterministic code is resolved with code. The LLM is used only where structured logic cannot substitute.

Testing combined automated and manual approaches in parallel, not sequentially. Automated suites ran continuously during development to catch regressions and lock behaviour contracts across all system layers. For example, test_ack_logic.py::test_terse_follow_up_after_validation_skips_repeat_ack caught subtle dialogue edge cases, while test_flow_hardening.py::test_intent_strategy_switches_to_transactional_for_budget_signal ensured FSM strategy switching logic was still intact even after changes made. These fast, repeatable checks prevented basic errors from persisting. Simultaneously, developers ran manual scenarios using test_detailed.py (a smoke–test harness) to observe real conversation flows and surface FSM issues automated scripts cannot detect–stage advancement sequences, natural dialogue progression and objection handling across multi–turn exchanges. This hand–in–hand approach caught logic errors early through automation while maintaining direct visibility into user–facing
Figure illustrates the three verification layers
All issues below were diagnosed during manual testing:

The model ended replies with deferential prompts like "Would you like to hear more?" – an RLHF politeness artefact (see Section 1.4) that undermines sales authority before value is established. The fix applied three layers: a soft prohibition in prompts.py:_SHARED_RULES, an FSM guard against premature pitch context and a regex post–processor as a final safety net.
Issue 2 – False stage advancement on weak signals.

analysis_config.yaml initially contained fragments such as "want to", "need to" and "trying" – phrases too vague to carry intent, causing the FSM to advance from Intent to Logical after a single turn, violating the NEPQ requirement to confirm intent through situational questioning first. The fix removed weak phrases, applied the word–boundary technique described in Section 3.1.3 to prevent substring matches and added the negation window detailed in Section 3.1.3 for phrases like "I'm not struggling." Vague conversations now remain in Intent for three to four turns before advancing.
Issue 3 – Over–probing and the interrogation effect.

Without a question limit, the model produced two to three questions per turn in the Logical stage; testers found this adversarial. Per Conversational Repair theory, rapid–fire questioning constitutes a turn–taking violation that provokes defensiveness (see Section 1.4). The fix added a "BE HUMAN" rule to _SHARED_RULES: one declarative observation must precede any question, with a hard cap of one question per reply.
Issue 4 – Premature pitching at Logical and Emotional stages.

Product–related keywords caused the model to pitch features before emotional stakes were established, bypassing NEPQ's Consequence of Inaction step (Miner, 2022). A HARD STOP block was added to the Logical and Emotional prompts and a guard in content.py:generate_stage_prompt() ensures pitch context is assembled only when current_stage == Stage.PITCH.
Refer to appendix Diary for evidence of iterative approach

It is recognised that a system–familiar developer cannot fully replicate the perspective of an unfamiliar user, nor guarantee coverage of edge cases that only emerge through novel, independent interaction.
The methodological choices were deliberately structured to balance ecological validity with measurable system validation. Remote Microsoft Teams sessions reduces “Hawthorne effect”

(McCambridge, Witton and Elbourne, 2014) because participants feel more comfortable in their own environments. Post–scenario interviews replace think–aloud protocol to prevent cognitive load that could affect natural task behaviour. Two fixed scenarios establish controlled conditions for comparable data and the third open–ended scenario deliberately surfaces edge–case behaviour that scripted prompts cannot expose. A deliberate split between sales–experienced and non–sales participants counters practitioner normalisation bias –experienced reps instinctively compensate for system gaps, while non–sales users reveal genuine usability and realism friction points. And having salespeople testing it makes sense as they will be actually using it. By having both, comparison can be done on such. Sample size of 10 used but with 4–6 used from each sales/non–sales background group, where this size is grounded in Nielsen's Law (Nielsen and Landauer, 1993) – ~85% of usability issues surface by N=5 with diminishing returns beyond.
In Section 4.3.2, a few figures are presented first. Then, analysis with clear design implications

Since 90% said this is for beginners, it is clear who the product is for. Actually this is a practical marketing insight: position this as a niche beginner–to–intermediate ramp–up tool, not a B2B Corporate training solution.

In addition, those with no sales experience (4/10) valued the structure and low–pressure environment. Those with sales experience (6/10) found it too easy and predictable, saying they could anticipate the chatbot's next move within a few exchanges. This reveals an interesting disparity to identify the audience the tool was built for those focusing on foundation–building, teaching the RAPPORT → DISCOVERY → PITCH flow, not advanced techniques. Having a visual flowchart of the conversation is potentially a “Could Have” or “Would Have” on MoSCoW prioritisation for the next iteration, intended for accelerated foundation–building.

Users unanimously praised the structured dialogue and said it was easy to use (4.1/5 and 4.6/5, respectively). But they rated its actual effectiveness at selling only 2.8/5. The problem is not the design, it is the execution. The system gets predictable. It accepts objections it should push back on. It generates verbose responses. It drags conversations on when it should wrap up. This means specific prompting will improve the responses

There’s a few missing features mentioned by participants. Six participants wanted measurable performance feedback and five wanted harder difficulty settings. These are actually signals that learners can't tell whether they're improving and aren't being potentially challenged enough to learn; hence, data regarding this needs to be tracked and placed at the priority of “Must Have” (MoSCoW prioritisation).
The clearest failure point is objection handling. When users pushed back ("I need to think about it," "I already use another tool”) the chatbot often accepted the deflection

rather than probing deeper. Around half of objection moments were mishandled this way. This suggested tighter control and a leading to the creation of a separate objection handling framework (objection.py)
26 automated tests confirm intent–lock prevents reversion; 9 tests validate FSM transition structure.
30 automated tests pass (config, difficulty injection, signal adaptation). Tone–alignment score not independently measured.
Real telemetry: avg 281ms, p95 1,433ms across 2,659 production conversations.
11 tests confirm rule injected in all strategies; 4 guardrail tests confirm output enforcement.
Deployed on Render free tier; Groq free API; Sambanova fallback verified.
29/29 tests pass (exceeds original 26 target): evaluation, generation, routing, scoring, fallback.
Training Coach: 61 tests pass (deterministic scoring across five dimensions). Expert–correlation validation out of scope.
In–memory architecture prioritised to meet O3 latency target on Render. Persistence deferred (Could Have, MoSCoW).

O2 and O7 are partially met: both features are fully implemented and tested, but independent validation metrics (tone–alignment %, expert–correlation score) were out of scope for the single–session UAT protocol.
O3 is the strongest result: 281ms average from 2,659 real conversations. O8 was a deliberate architectural trade–off prioritising sub–2000ms latency over cross–session persistence.
4.5 Current System Limitations and Failure Boundaries
Limitations are categorised by addressability: some arise from the inherent nature of LLMs and cannot be eliminated, while others are addressable and represent deliberate scope decisions.

Language models are probabilistic. Identical prompts yield different responses across runs. This is not a bug–it is the architecture. Consequence: The chatbot cannot guarantee identical behaviour in every situation, but staged constraints (YAML advancement rules, override checks) reduce variance to acceptable levels for training use.
: Across 170 domain tests with fixed prompts, completion rate variance ≈ ±2% turn–to–turn. Advancement stage remains consistent 94% of the time due to deterministic FSM rules.

The LLM operates on a finite context window (~4k tokens per conversation). Extended conversations compress older context, potentially losing earlier problem statements. Mitigation: Session summaries are injected before each API call to maintain problem continuity.
Sessions stored in memory; lost on server restart
Trade–off: File–based persistence adds latency (~50ms per write); in–memory is acceptable for training tool
Pattern–matching filters only; regex does not catch all injection attempts
Sufficient for this scope (non–adversarial, controlled trainer input); not suitable for public APIs
No horizontal scaling; max ~50 concurrent sessions before degradation
Architectural choice: Flask app is stateless; sessions in–memory breaks distributed design
No retry logic; Groq timeout → immediate API error returned to user
Groq uptime 99.8% (audited); retries add 2–3s per failure; not justified for training context
Implement exponential backoff + fallback provider queue
Ambiguous phrasings (e.g., "maybe later" vs. "I need to think") remain misclassified
Would require LLM–based classifier (adds 300ms latency per turn); keyword heuristic is 85% accurate for clear intent
Acceptable for training; retrain classifier for production
Edge cases in objection reframing (6/170 tests involved manual expectation–setting, not automated assertions)
Quiz scoring fallback (LLM failure → score 50); no keyword–based fallback implemented
Prospect mode scoring (deterministic keyword–based scoring; no A/B ground truth from real salespeople)

Inherent LLM constraints (variance, context limits) are well–understood and mitigated where possible. Addressable gaps (session storage, scaling, input validation) are scoped due to time, cost or quality trade–offs.

Sales ethics represents one of the most enduring tensions in professional practice. Hartmann et al. (2023) confirm that the intersection of sales and ethics is "vast and, therefore, difficult to summarize" spanning over four decades of unresolved scholarly debate – precisely because the same techniques that build trust can equally exploit it.

The individual salesperson's moral judgment sits at the centre of this debate. Ingram et al. (2007) argue that “enhancing the moral behaviour of salespeople is increasingly important, but difficult " given that sales management decisions directly shape frontline ethical conduct.

The system is deployed as a supplementary practice tool, designed to reward consultative behaviours – specifically active listening, open–ended questioning and empathy, rather than high–pressure or directive interaction patterns. Stages cannot advance without customer–signal detection, structurally incentivising discovery over persuasion.

Voice mode would benefit most from paralinguistic analysis: real–time detection of filler words, talk–to–listen ratio and pacing against stage norms would surface coaching signals unavailable in text–only sessions, extending the framework’s feedback depth into delivery as well as content.

Performance persistence is a further priority: persisting analytics per user profile across sessions would enable the system to surface each practitioner’s highest–leverage improvement areas and track longitudinal progress, converting one–off practice into a structured development pathway.

A sales call analysis feature, enabling users to upload or record real calls for automated framework–aligned feedback, would extend the system’s value beyond simulated practice into live performance review.

A gamification layer would introduce XP awarded per session scaled by performance score, unlocking difficulty tiers and prospect variants as practitioners improve, with a team leaderboard surfacing rankings by weekly XP and stage progression to introduce the social accountability and motivation of an in–person sales floor. Crucially, all required inputs already exist in the deterministic scoring pipeline (score_session()), meaning implementation requires only a persistence layer and lightweight UI–making this among the highest–ROI extensions for a future iteration.

I front–loaded too much time on designing the initial architecture before any implementation had been tested. Given that I naturally work through trial and error and manual testing, this was a poor fit for how I actually think. A tighter design–implement cycle from the start would have moved the project forward faster and grounded early decisions in real evidence rather than untested assumptions.
A tighter build–test–redesign loop would have served the project better from the outset.

revisit any technical decision, it would be the move from keyword–matching to a cloud API provider. It would have been interesting to push further with keyword–matching (as this would be far more deterministic), augmenting it with NLP techniques for tighter framework adherence and introduced the LLM later purely as a corrective layer to pull conversations back on–track when they drifted or edge–cases. And in prospect–mode, LLM used only to create variance and unique feel.

I developed a durable skill in controlled, structured and academically–backed prompt engineering while rooting myself in applying behavioural psychology. Given the rapid development of the AI industry, this is a skill I will carry forward.
The New Model of Selling: Selling to an Unsellable Generation
. New York, NY: Morgan James Publishing.

Detailed failure–to–fix traces and code snippets supporting Section 2.1.
Questionnaire instrument, raw responses, summary statistics and transcript excerpts.
Intent–lock prevents stage reversion; frustration override; literal–question detection; context–aware guardedness; four integration scenarios
FSM transition matrix; shared pitch/objection; advancement–condition isolation
Persona config loading, difficulty injection, state management, prompt
"Would you like?" ban injected in all strategies
Final safety check: pricing block, multi–question reduction, guardrail integration
Quiz generation, scoring, routing, rubric loading, hybrid fallback
Training Coach generation, answer routing, session scoring, signal detection
Total: 94/94 tests passing. All metrics collected. Verified 22 April 2026.
